coredata.dc:title: Developing B, P doped carbon dot with Long-Lifetime aggregation-induced room temperature phosphorescence for sensing metronidazole, information encryption and anticounterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Xiaoye
Wang, Yan
Pang, Chenjie
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted much attention from researchers. It has the peculiarity of longer luminescence lifetime, large Stokes shift and environmental sensitivity. In this work, a design strategy of longer luminescence lifetime RTP materials was constructed based on B, P co-doped CDs (BCND), in combination with a strategy for the suppression of the non-radiative transitions of the triple-state excitons of the phosphorescent. The BCND were prepared by solvothermal method with many heteroatoms doped to enhance the spin–orbit coupling of CDs, thereby regulating their luminescence properties. The aggregation-induced phosphorescence (AIP) properties of BCND were investigated in the mixed solvent of N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and H2O, and the luminescence mechanism was discussed in detail. The BCND emited blue fluorescence (FL) with quantum yield of 9.85 % and lifetime of 6.30 ns, and it emited green RTP after the UV irradiation turned off, which can last about 10 s to the naked eye. The RTP quantum yield and lifetime are 18.7 % and 1.33 s, respectively. Based on two different emission properties (FL and RTP) and the good water dispersibility of BCND, the study was successfully carried out for the detection of metronidazole (MNZ) in drugs and fingerprint recognition and information security encryption. The preparation of CDs with AIP properties in this research initiated a new thought for the development of RTP nanoparticles with long lifetime in aqueous solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced room-temperature phosphorescence
Metronidazole
Fingerprint recognition
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of BCND with RTP and BCND0 without RTP
Determination of fluorescence and phosphorescence quantum yield of BCND
Quantitative detection of MNZ based the BCND
Pretreatment and detection of MNZ tablets
Preparation of phosphorescent and non-phosphorescent inks based the BCND
Aggregation-induced room-temperature phosphorescence property of BCND2
Study on the detection mechanism of MNZ
Study on the detection of MNZ in real samples
Fingerprint recognition and anti-counterfeiting applications of BCND2

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of BCND1, BCND2, and BCND3 powders under the UV irradiation (365 nm) off from 1-12 s.

(a) Solid fluorescence lifetime of BCND2; (b) phosphorescence lifetime; (c) fluorescence excitation spectra I; fluorescence emission spectra II of BCND2; UV absorption spectra III in aqueous solution; (d) fluorescence emission spectra of BCND2 under different excitation wavelength.
(a) TEM image of BCND2, (b) XRD spectra of BCND2, (c) FTIR spectra of BCND2, and (d) XPS spectra of BCND2.
Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of BCND2 in DMF-H2O mixed solutions (a, c) and Fluorescent and phosphorescence image under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation and off (b, d).

(a) The Fluorescence spectra of BCND2, BCND2 + MNZ and MNZ (Inserted fluorescent images: I-BCND2, II-BCND2 + MNZ, III-MNZ); (b) linear relationship between RTP of BCND2 and the different concentration of MNZ (70–800 μM) (c)70–450 μM and (d)450–800 μM, respectively.
Selectivity and anti-interference of BCND2 for sensing MNZ.
(a) UV absorption spectra of BCND2 (I) and MNZ (III), fluorescence emission spectra of BCND2 (II) (b) Fluorescence lifetime of BCND2 and BCND2-MNZ.
The IFE quenching mechanism of BCND2 for sensing MNZ.
(a) Image of BCND2 in solution with UV lamp on/off; (b) Fingerprint identification images with different concentrations; (c) Information “2023” encrypted image.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of BCND2, luminescence mechanism, detection for MNZ and application of BCND2 with RTP.
Calculates the IFE parameters of BCND2-MNZ system.

head.ce:title: Developing B, P doped carbon dot with Long-Lifetime aggregation-induced room temperature phosphorescence for sensing metronidazole, information encryption and anticounterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006 Shanxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, Shanxi, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xiaoye Wen and Yan Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of BCND2, luminescence mechanism, detection for MNZ and application of BCND2 with RTP.

Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted much attention from researchers. It has the peculiarity of longer luminescence lifetime, large Stokes shift and environmental sensitivity. In this work, a design strategy of longer luminescence lifetime RTP materials was constructed based on B, P co-doped CDs (BCND), in combination with a strategy for the suppression of the non-radiative transitions of the triple-state excitons of the phosphorescent. The BCND were prepared by solvothermal method with many heteroatoms doped to enhance the spin–orbit coupling of CDs, thereby regulating their luminescence properties. The aggregation-induced phosphorescence (AIP) properties of BCND were investigated in the mixed solvent of N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and H2O, and the luminescence mechanism was discussed in detail. The BCND emited blue fluorescence (FL) with quantum yield of 9.85 % and lifetime of 6.30 ns, and it emited green RTP after the UV irradiation turned off, which can last about 10 s to the naked eye. The RTP quantum yield and lifetime are 18.7 % and 1.33 s, respectively. Based on two different emission properties (FL and RTP) and the good water dispersibility of BCND, the study was successfully carried out for the detection of metronidazole (MNZ) in drugs and fingerprint recognition and information security encryption. The preparation of CDs with AIP properties in this research initiated a new thought for the development of RTP nanoparticles with long lifetime in aqueous solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have recently attracted the attention of numerous researchers, due to their advantageous characteristic, including a large Stokes shift, long lifetime, and effective removal of autofluorescence and self-absorption [1–4]. The RTP material typically exhibits the lifetime of a few seconds to hours when the excitation light was turned off. It can be widely applied to chem-sensing, bioimaging, optoelectronics, information security and so on [5–9]. At present, the research of RTP materials normally focus on the containing metal-inorganic material or organometallic compounds [10–13]. these raw materials may be high cost and toxic. In addition, the successful application of phosphorescence imaging was limited, due to their poor biocompatibility and water dispersion. Some non-metallized RTP materials have been reported [14–17], but due to the presence of large non-radiative constants and weak spin–orbit coupling (SOC), as well as the potential for triplet excitation light to be deactivated by numerous non-radiative decay processes, further development is required. Consequently, it is necessary to develop non-metallized RTP materials with long-lived, low toxicology and excellent aqueous dispersion.

Carbon dots (CDs) have rich energy levels and huge light fields, which can be applied in organism fields, photoelectric materials and chemical detection due to its good solubility in aqueous solution, simple synthesis process, excellent light stability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [18–26]. Therefore, it was a potential candidate for RTP materials. To achieve efficient CDs-based RTP materials, many strategic were considered. For example, the polymer CDs can experience cross-linked enhanced emission (CEE) effect based on the restraint of non-radiative transitions, resulting in enhanced RTP properties [27,28]. And functionalized CDs materials with various substrates such as silica gel, urea, polyurethane, zeolite and so on, can enhance the phosphorescence properties [29–32]. However, the majority of reported CDs with RTP properties are limited to their solid application [33–38]. These materials are susceptible to quenching by H2O and O2. Notwithstanding, some notable attempts have been reported to construct water-soluble CDs with RTP properties [39–41]. However, most water-soluble CDs with RTP properties display a relatively short lifetime and are susceptible to quenching in high-water solvents, which constrains their potential applications. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) phenomenon has attracted more and more researchers since it was discovered by Tang [42]. It was the feasible strategy for developing effective RTP materials with AIE property [43–45]. Furthermore, the rigid structure of the AIE molecular can increase intersystem crossing (ISC) and achieve long-lived RTP materials [46,47].

Here, B, P co-doped CDs (BCND) with long-lifetime RTP property was prepared. The BCND can emit blue fluorescence and green RTP, and the RTP quantum yield and lifetime are 8.17 % and 1.33 s, respectively. The green RTP can sustain about 10 s to the naked eye after UV lamp turned off (Scheme 1 ). Furthermore, the aggregation-induced phosphorescence (AIP) properties of BCND in DMF and H2O mixed solvent were investigated. Based on fluorescence and RTP properties and good water dispersibility of BCND, the fluorescence system for detecting of metronidazole in drugs by internal rate effect (IFE) was achieved, and the mechanism of detection has been discussed in depth. Furthermore, the BCND was successfully applied to fingerprint recognition and information security encryption. The preparation of CDs with RTP properties in this research initiated a new thought for the development of aqueous RTP nanoparticles with longer lifetime.
Seen Supporting Materials for details.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BCND with RTP and BCND0 without RTP
ce:section.ce:para: BCND was prepared by hydrothermal method (Scheme 2 ). First, boric acid (1.5 g) was added in 20 mL of H2O. Then triethylene tetramine (0.8 mL) and different amounts of phosphoric acid (1.3, 1.6, 1.8 mL) were added. In a reactor vessel (50 mL), the resultant dispersion was added and reacted at 200 °C for 6.0 h. The obtained powder samples were dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water, dialyzed with a 500 Dw dialysis bag for 6 h, and freeze-dried to obtain a purified solid powder sample (BCND).

BCND0 was prepared by hydrothermal method [48]. 3-triamino-propyl trimethoxysilane (2.3 mL) and H2O (10 mL) were mixed, then added to a reactor, heated at 250 °C for 10 h. The obtained powder samples were dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water, dialyzed with a 500 Dw dialysis bag for 6 h, and freeze-dried to obtain a purified solid powder sample (BCND0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of fluorescence and phosphorescence quantum yield of BCND
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of the BCND was measured according to the previous literature method [49]. Quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the reference standard, the quantum yield of quinine sulfate at 350 nm was 0.577, and the quantum yield was determined in accordance with the Equation (1):
(1)
Q =
Q s
η
η s
2
I
I s
A s
A

Where Q is the quantum yield of BCND, A is the absorbance, I is the fluorescence/phosphor emission intensity at a specific excitation wavelength, and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “s” represents the reference standard for the quantum yield. In order to reduce the influence of reabsorption in the sample solution for the emission spectrum, the absorbance value (A) of all solutions is always controlled below 0.1.

The phosphorescence quantum yield of the BCND was determined according to Equation (2):
(2)
η =
ε α
=
∫ 350
L
e m i s s i o n
∫
E
r e f e r e n c e
- ∫
E
s a m p l e

Where ε is the number of photons emitted by the sample collected by the integrating sphere within the range of 350 nm, α is the number of photons absorbed by the sample, Lemission is the net emission of the sample, Ereference is the absorption spectrum of the reference in the integrating sphere, and Esample is the absorption spectrum of the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of MNZ based the BCND
ce:section.ce:para: MNZ with different concentrations was mixed with BCND (10 mg/mL) and Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 8.0). H2O was added to obtain a final mixture of 5 mL. The FL emission spectra were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and detection of MNZ tablets
ce:section.ce:para: The MNZ tablets purchased from a local pharmacy in Taiyuan was grinded into powder. Then, MNZ tablets (50 mg) was dissolved in 5 mL H2O, and treated ultrasound for 5 min. Then, the mixture was strained with filter paper, and set its volume to 10 mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of phosphorescent and non-phosphorescent inks based the BCND
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the phosphorescent ink, BCND (100 mg) and H2O (1 mL) were mixed, then glycerin (1 mL) was added, stirred evenly at 40 ℃, and cooled for use. Under the same experimental procedure, the non-phosphorescent ink based BCND0 was prepared.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image and corresponding size distribution displayed that the BCND2 consisted of well-dispersed particles with an average size of 1.62 nm (Fig. 3 a). The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of BCND2 has a peak at 22.7°, which is a typical peak with disordered carbon atoms resulting from covalent cross-linked frameworks (Fig. 3b). The functional groups of the BCND2 were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 3c). BCND2 has obvious absorption forms in the range of 2827 ∼ 3418 cm−1, which was attributable to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl group, amino group and methylene group. The absorption bonds were obtained at 1636, 1530 and 1467 cm−1, which was attributable to the stretching vibration of the C=C bond, N-H bond and C-N bond, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 1184, 997, and 821 cm−1 were observed from the stretching vibrations form of the P=O, N-P and B-O bonds, respectively. The study of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) also verified the results of FTIR analysis and investigated the elemental composition of the BCND2 (Fig. 3d). The C1s spectrum of BCND2 was divided into two peaks, which can be the were assigned to the C-C/C=C (284.6 eV) and C-N/C-P (286.1 eV) (Fig. S2a). And the two peaks of N1s at 399.4 eV and 401.3 eV, were corresponded to the C-N=C bond and the N-P bond (Fig. S2b). Likewise, The O1s spectrum was fitted as three peaks at 531.1, 531.9 and 532.9 eV, which may be ascribed to the C-O, P-O, B-O bonds, respectively (Fig. S2c). The B1s spectrum consists of two peaks at 192.4 and 190.8 eV, which were related to the B-O and B-N bonds, respectively (Fig. S2d). The P 2p spectrum was fitted with two peaks at binding energies of 133.3 and 133.9 eV, which can be assigned to the P–O/P=O and P–N bonds (Fig. S2e). The XPS spectra of BCND2 had five typical elements peaks: O 1 s (531.3 eV); N 1 s (399.2 eV); C 1 s (284.4 eV); B 1 s (184.1 eV) and P 2p (133.1 eV). All the results demonstrated that the BCND2 were successfully doped with the elements of N, P and B. Additional, using 0.1 mM quinine sulfate as a reference, the fluorescence quantum yield of BCND2 was calculated to be 9.85 %, and the phosphorescence quantum yield was 18.7 % using integrating sphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation-induced room-temperature phosphorescence property of BCND2
ce:section.ce:para: In order to realize the potential applications of BCND2, its optical stability was investigated under different conditions, including different pH values and UV exposure times. As shown in Fig. S3a, the fluorescence intensity of BCND2 remained almost unchanged in the pH range from 3 to 10, indicating its good resistance to acid and alkali. The fluorescence intensity of BCND2 remained stable after 60 min of irradiation with the 365 nm UV light (Fig. S3b), and it exhibited strong fluorescence intensity when irradiated at 2 min, indicated that BCND2 had good anti-photobleaching property. All the results demonstrated that the BCND2 displayed the excellent optical stability. The experimental conditions for BCND2 were optimized as the Tris-HCl buffer solution with pH = 7.4 for 10 min. The AIE and AIP properties of BCND2 were investigated in the DMF and Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.4) mixed solvents. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of BCND2 increased gradually with the varying the volume fractions of DMF (fdDMF), which may be attributed to the strengthening of van der Waals force and hydrogen bonding of BCND2 in mixed solution, resulted in limited the vibration of particles and reduced the non-radiative transition [44]. The BCND2 showed no RTP emission in Tris-HCl buffer solution (fdDMF = 0), and the BCND2 maybe susceptible to quenching of triple exciton due to vibration and oxygen mediated. With fdDMF increased, the phosphorescence of BCND2 appeared and enhanced gradually, which was attributed to the incipient aggregates form of BCND2 and lights up the RTP due to the protection of rigid structure. The RTP of BCND2 exhibited enhanced significantly, which immobilize the triplet state and inhibit the intermolecular motion based on the rich surface functional groups on the surface of BCND2 and the interaction in the mixed solution, thus effectively improving the long lifetime of RTP [50].

Based on the good optical properties of BCND2, the detection of MNZ was successfully established. The fluorescence of BCND2 was quenched when the MNZ was added, which can be observed by naked eye under the UV (365 nm) irradiation (Fig. 5 a). The detection conditions of BCND2-MNZ system were measured., the FL quenching effect of BCND2 by MNZ remained almost unchanged in the pH range from 3 to 10 (Fig. S4). And the quenching time was investigated, the quenching efficiency reached the highest value quickly and not change significantly with the increase of time at 2–60 min. As a result, pH = 7.4 and incubation time of 10 min were identified as the optimal detection conditions. The FL of BCND2 continuously diminished with the concentration of MNZ increasing from 50 μM to 800 μM (Fig. 5b). It can be found that there are two linear ranges for the fluorescence signal declined (Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d). Consequently, the linear equations were y = 82399.35–132.94x with R2 = 0.990 and y = 42273.30–43.99x with R2 = 0.987, respectively, and the detection limit was calculated to be 9.699 μM and 29.31 μM (3σ/k, n = 11), respectively. The correlation coefficients of the two linear fitting equations showed that BCND2 was more practical to detect MNZ. On the other hand, the effect of possible interfering substances (glucose, ascorbic acid, glutathione, L-cysteine and various ions Mn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, K+, SO4 2−) for the detection of MNZ were studied. As shown in Fig. 6 , the MNZ was added to BCND2 solution, and the possible interfering substances were added successively. The fluorescence of BCND2 in the presence and absence of these interfering substances was recorded. The experimental results demonstrated that the fluorescence of BCND2-MNZ system was almost unaffected by other interfering substances, indicating that BCND2 has good anti-interference ability and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the detection mechanism of MNZ
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, internal filtration effect (IFE), energy resonance transfer (FRET) and photoelectron transfer (PET) can cause fluorescence quenching of fluorescent materials [51]. It can be studied from the UV absorption spectra of BCND2, MNZ and the fluorescence spectra of BCND2 that the absorption peak of BCND2 was located at 350 nm, the absorption peak of MNZ was located at 320 nm. There was no overlap between the emission peak of BCND2 and the absorption peak of MNZ (Fig. 7 a). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching was not caused by FRET. In order to investigate the quenching mechanism of BCND2, the FL lifetime of BCND2 was determined to be 7.62 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime of BCND2-MNZ was 7.50 ns (Fig. 7b, Table S4). The lifetimes of BCND2 were the same basically, demonstrating that the quenching mechanisms was not PET [52]. Finally, the IFE effect was considered for the FL quenching of BCND2-MNZ. (Fig. 8 ). The IFE effect does not belong to the static or dynamic quenching process, so the absorption peaks of the BCND2 cannot change, furthermore indicating that there was not any new substance appeared in the system. As a result, the lifetime of BCND2 will not be changed before and after added MNZ. In addition, the Parker equation was further studied the hypothesis for the IFE process (Fig. S5) [53]:
(3)
CF =
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em
where d is the width of the colorimetric cup (1.0 cm) and s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.1 cm). g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the colorimetric cup (0.4 cm). Fcor is the fluorescence intensity corrected after filtering IFE effect, Fobsd is the observed fluorescence intensity, Aex and Aem are the absorbance values at the optimum excitation wavelength emission wavelength. The absorbance and fluorescence intensity of BCND2 were studied after different concentrations of MNZ were added. First, the correction factor (CF) was calculated according to Equation (3). Secondly, the fluorescence efficiency Fcor⁄Fcor.0 was calculated. With the concentrations of MNZ increased, the value of CF increased from 1.30 to 1.53, The result was consistent with the Parker equation, it was also found that the fluorescence inhibition rate of MNZ for BCND2 was as high as 98 %, indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of BCND2 was IFE (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the detection of MNZ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the feasibility of BCND2 for the determination of MNZ, the concentration of MNZ in metronidazole tablets was analyzed by standard addition method. And the recoveries of MNZ in the samples were in the range of 101.5 %-104.5 % with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) in the range of 0.99 %-3.0 %. The prepared BCND2 can be successfully applied to the determination of MNZ in tablet samples, which provides a rapid, simple and accurate method for the detection of MNZ in real samples (Table S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fingerprint recognition and anti-counterfeiting applications of BCND2
ce:section.ce:para: The good AIP properties of BCND2 were utilized to study its practical applications in fingerprint identification and anti-counterfeiting. The solution of BCND2 was prepared in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4) mixed with 50 % DMF. The BCND2 displayed bright blue FL under UV (365 nm) lamp and emitted green RTP for 10 s when the UV irradiation was turned off (Fig. 9 a). Simultaneously, the fingerprints dealt with BCND2 ink could be clearly observed the bright blue FL with UV (365 nm) irradiation and the green RTP with UV off (Fig. 9b). And the fingerprints images could be better observed when the concentration of BCND2 was 24 mg (Fig. S6), indicating that BCND2 exhibited good lipophilicity and the small nano-size.

Furthermore, the prepared BCND2 nanomaterials were applied in optical anti-counterfeiting to realize advanced coded encryption based on its unique AIP properties. The error information “8888” encoded together by the BCND2 and BCND0 link. When irradiated with 365 nm UV lamp, the number “8888” was observed with blue fluorescence, and with the UV light turned off, the luminescent color of the marked changes from blue to green color due to the AIP properties of BCND2, which made it easy to identify the encrypted information of “2023” (Fig. 9c). The experimental results demonstrated that the fluorescent and phosphorescence emission of BCND2 was promote to the fulfilment of anti-counterfeiting and fingerprint identification, which can further improve the information security.

In this study, we proposed a strategy to prepare B, P co-doped CDs (BCND2) with aggregation-induced room-temperature phosphorescence, which has fluorescence quantum yield of 9.85 % with a lifetime of 6.30 ns, and phosphorescence quantum yield as high as 18.7 % with lifetime up to 1.33 s. And it can even be observed by the naked eye for 10 s of green phosphorescence. Based on the fluorescence and phosphorescence properties of BCND2, the fluorescence system for detecting MNZ based on BCND2 has been successfully established, and the mechanism of detection has been discussed in depth. In addition, BCND2 has been used as anti-counterfeiting ink in the field of fingerprint identification and information encryption.

Xiaoye Wen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yan Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Chenjie Pang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhefeng Fan: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113155.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous promotion of efficiency and lifetime of organic phosphorescence for self-referenced temperature sensing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clustering-triggered emission and persistent room temperature phosphorescence of sodium alginate

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intercrossed carbon nanorings with pure surface states as low-cost and environment-friendly phosphors for white-light- emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.M. Li, Y.L. Liu, X.F. Song, H. Wang, H.S. Gu, H.B. Zeng. Intercrossed carbon nanorings with pure surface states as low-cost and environment-friendly phosphors for white-light- emitting diodes, Angew Chem, Int Ed 54 (2015) 1759-1764, https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201406836.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable afterglow andself-trapped exciton emissions in Zr (IV)-based organic-inorganic metal halide hybrids by metal-ion doping
